title: Module 2 quiz — Helpful, Reliable, People-First Content

questions.prompt: A consultant tells you that 'improving your E-E-A-T score' will fix your rankings. What's the most accurate response?

choices.text: Great, sign me up — E-E-A-T is the #1 ranking factor.

There's no E-E-A-T score in Google's algorithm. E-E-A-T is a framework quality raters use; the algorithm uses many separate signals (bylines, citations, authority, etc.) that correlate with it. Ask them which specific signals they'll improve.
E-E-A-T only matters for medical and financial sites — skip it.
Ask them for the AI-generated E-E-A-T audit report.

questions.explanation: Google explicitly says 'E-E-A-T itself isn't a specific ranking factor.' Any service offering should be able to name the *specific signals* they're improving. 'E-E-A-T optimization' as a vague service is vapor — concrete signal improvements (bylines, source citations, author pages, primary research) are real work.

questions.prompt: You're auditing one of your blog posts using the Who/How/Why framework. The Why answer is: 'this exists primarily to capture search traffic for the keyword X.' What does Google's guidance suggest?

choices.text: Good — that's the right Why for SEO.
Wrong Why. Google's systems are designed to discount content created primarily to attract search traffic rather than to help users. Either rewrite the page to serve a real reader, or kill it.
Add more keywords to the title to strengthen the Why.
Add a stronger CTA at the end to convert the traffic.

questions.explanation: Google's helpful content guidance is explicit: 'If you're primarily making content to attract search engine visits, that's not aligned with what our systems seek to reward.' The Why has a built-in lie detector — if your content wouldn't exist without Google, Google increasingly won't rank it. Rewrite around a real audience or cull.

questions.prompt: Which of these is the clearest example of non-commodity content?

choices.text: 'Complete beginner's guide to investing' — covers everything generally.

'I lost $40,000 in my first year of options trading. Here's the exact playbook I used, what went wrong, and the four rules I now follow' — first-hand experience with specific outcomes and a defensible take.
'10 tips to invest better' — generic listicle.
'What is a stock?' — beginner-level explainer.

questions.explanation: Non-commodity content has specifics (the dollar amount), first-hand experience (the year of trading), a defensible angle (what went wrong + the four rules), and information that wouldn't exist without this specific author. The other options are commodity — generic, widely available information that any decent AI could produce.

questions.prompt: You publish one article about medication dosages on your otherwise non-medical site. It ranks well for a year, then suddenly drops out of results after a quality update. What's the most likely cause?

choices.text: Random algorithm fluctuation — it'll come back on its own.

The article is a YMYL page on a non-YMYL site. Likely missing: credentialed author/reviewer, primary medical citations, recent update date. YMYL pages need YMYL-grade treatment even when they live on non-YMYL sites.
Probably keyword stuffing.
Probably bad backlinks.

questions.explanation: Medication dosing is squarely YMYL — Google applies heightened E-E-A-T scrutiny. Even a single YMYL page on a non-YMYL site needs credentials, primary citations, and visible review. Add 'Reviewed by [credentialed person]', cite primary sources, keep it updated, or remove it.

questions.prompt: Which AI-content pattern most clearly triggers Google's scaled content abuse policy?

choices.text: Using AI to summarize source papers as part of human-led research.
Using AI to draft sections that a knowledgeable human then heavily revises and verifies.
Publishing hundreds of templated articles per month targeting keyword variations with minimal human input.
Disclosing that AI helped draft an article that a credentialed human reviewed.

questions.explanation: The policy targets *many pages × low value × intent to manipulate rankings*. AI alone isn't the trigger. Mass-produced, templated, minimally-reviewed content at volume is exactly what the policy was written for — and it's also the pattern most likely to cause a catastrophic core update loss.
